g.data-narration: Data structures are ways of organizing and storing data for efficient access and modification.
g.text: Data structures are ways of organizing and storing data in a computer so that
it can be accessed and modified efficiently. They form the foundation of

g.data-narration: Data structures are classified into two main categories: primitive and non-primitive types.
Primitive data structures are basic data types built into programming languages.
g.text: • Predefined by programming language • Fixed size • Direct machine instruction support
• Simple representation of single values • Fundamental building blocks

g.data-narration: Integer represents whole numbers. Typical sizes are 2, 4, or 8 bytes depending on the system.
g.text: Represents whole numbers - positive, negative, or zero.

g.data-narration: Float represents real numbers with decimal points. Float is single precision, double is double precision.
g.text: Represents real numbers with decimal points.

g.data-narration: Character stores single characters (1 byte), Boolean stores true/false values for logic operations.
Pointers store memory addresses of other variables, enabling dynamic memory allocation.
g.text: Stores memory addresses of other variables.

g.data-narration: Non-primitive data structures are derived from primitive types, complex, and have variable size.
g.text: • User-defined or library-defined • Store collections of data • Variable size
• Complex structure • Dynamic memory allocation possible

g.data-narration: Arrays store elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations with fast O(1) random access.
g.text: Collection of same-type elements in contiguous memory.

g.data-narration: Linked lists consist of nodes with data and pointers, offering dynamic size with O(1) insertion/deletion.
g.text: Nodes with data + pointer to next node. Dynamic size.
✓ Dynamic size, O(1) insert/delete | ✗ Sequential access O(n), extra memory for pointers

g.data-narration: Stacks follow LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) principle with push and pop operations.
g.text: Last-In-First-Out: Push (add) to top, Pop (remove) from top.
Applications: Function calls, Undo, Expression eval
Used in: Browser history, Backtracking algorithms, Call stack management

g.data-narration: Queues follow FIFO (First-In-First-Out) principle. Enqueue adds to rear, dequeue removes from front.
g.text: First-In-First-Out: Enqueue (add) at rear, Dequeue (remove) from front.

g.data-narration: Trees are hierarchical structures with root at top, branches downward to leaf nodes.
g.text: Hierarchical with root at top, parent-child relationships, leaf nodes at bottom.
